title: Katya Luscomb -Hiring Manager Interview - Andi | Technical Managing Editor
meeting_title: Katya Luscomb -Hiring Manager Interview - Andi | Technical Managing Editor

transcript.text: Hi, Katya.
Hi, Andi.
How are you?
Good.
How are you?
Good.
Thanks.
Nice to meet you.
Thanks for scheduling some time.
Yeah.
Nice to meet you, too.
I work central time, even though I live Pacific, so it's my little 30-minute lunch break.
Oh, nice.
Okay.
That's kind of nice.
You end earlier, then?
Yeah, I end at 2 every day, which is really nice.
Lovely.
Yeah.
Perfect.
Well, I am Andi.
I lead customer operations for GrowthX.
Thanks.
I can start by giving you a bit.
A of background on how I'm thinking about the role and how it fits within GrowthX.
And then we can talk a little bit about you, and then I'll try and leave 10-ish minutes at the end for you to ask questions.
Perfect.
So customer operations at GrowthX basically means that I help organize the delivery team, which is what we call our sort of client-facing organization.
So thinking about like processes, tools, and how all of that comes together to get our deliverables out on time, sort of meet, contract, SoW, things like that.
So this role is kind of the technical, I mean, I would say is like the core piece of every pod in the delivery team.
You are...
The middleman in a lot of ways.

And so that means that there's like two lanes that you really have to execute in to be successful, which the first is thinking about, you know, project management writ large, both client facing and internally.
Um, so we have, the pods are made up of a account director, account strategist, um, who's kind of setting the long-term vision, making sure that the road ahead is planned out.
Um, and the project management piece is making sure that like every week we're kind of hitting the goals that we set and moving towards that like long-term vision.

Um, and then the other person on the team that you're working with, um, is a content manager, um, and content managers are basically running, um, the AI workflows that are generating the content on a day-to-day basis.
Um, so each client we're generating.
Generating five to 10 pieces of content a week, sometimes more if it's like really programmatic, and our workflows are like, you know, they can generate a ton at a time, give like, depending.
So the content manager is kind of in the tool every day running the workflows.
But this person is project managing, kind of editing things before they go out, making sure that things are going out on time, and speaking with the client, syncing with the account team, like pod.
To make sure all that's happening.
The other side is, you know, because the content manager is in the tool every day, you're also working a lot in the platform as well.
So growthx has its own internal platform that we have built, it's two months old, relatively new, but it's kind of incredible what it can do.
So, you know, there are templated workflows, and then...
We kind of customize them as we start to learn more about the client and if we need to adjust strategy, we can shift workflows or build a new one.
And so because it's still new and because there's a lot of customization that can happen for individual clients, we want somebody who can also help facilitate the feedback loop.

You know, content managers are great at kind of pushing out day-to-day pieces of content, but we want an ME's help in thinking about like writ large, where we improve the platform and the workflows to make it more effective and efficient as we're producing that content.
So, I mean, obviously, GrowthX is thinking a lot about how we scale.
We've grown very quickly so far, and hopefully we'll continue to do so.
And so we need to like, we can't hire.
We're like three people every time we have a new client.
And so we need to keep improving these workflows, keep making sure that we can have those efficiency gains over time.

And this role, we're hoping that someone can think really strategically about prompts and what works and doesn't within a workflow so that our engineering team can kind of keep working on making that better.
Any questions?
No.
Helpful additional detail.
I talked a fair bit with Marila.
I think I'm pronouncing that right.
Yeah.
A couple of weeks ago.
Yeah.
And so it's helpful to hear a little bit more specifics about how this role would interface with the pods and kind of with the client.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Helpful so far.
Thanks.
Perfect.
Okay.
So let's talk about you.
Why don't you tell me a little bit about yourself?
Yeah.
So I have a pretty varied background.
I don't
How much you've gotten to explore my resume and experience.
My most recent experience, I work as a content analyst and editor for a giant tech AI company.
I can't go into too much detail of MBAs and things.
But I do a lot of fact-checking and editorial work to train these large AI models on tone and fact and making sure they're pulling from reputable places on the web to produce their content.
And so that really is like the writing side of me, of my career.
I've also done a lot of like outdoor education project management, where I really like to dive into that sort of process thinking that you're hitting on that this role would also involve.
So I've done kind of a catch-all of things in that capacity.
I managed a hiring team several years ago for a small camp up in California, where we hired like 60 people every season to...

And so I managed, you know, the different layers of recruitment and also making sure that our onboarding teams were on track, talking to some of our high profile hires that we wanted to, like, really put an emphasis on, but also training some of our other recruiting team members on how to have those conversations to, you know, keep candidates engaged and all of that.
Um, yeah, I, my writing side, um, I have, uh, getting a little nervous, sorry.
Um, I have a pretty varied background.
So I was in communications in college, um, dove into like ran our student newspaper, um, which led me to some journalistic experience.

Uh, I worked for a summer with the Pittsburgh Post-Gazette, um, where I did a lot with their news desk, um, wrote some different blog posts and things like that, um, as well as some articles that were published in their main paper.
So I'm familiar with kind of that journalistic editorial process of, you know, having a pitch and doing.
research and curating and editing for tone and voice and style.
And then I've done some more in-depth writing and research as well for a website that I like to describe it as a written masterclass on well-being and different psychology topics.
And so all of us who are writing for it did a really deep dive into these various topics.

And that was an interesting project because the director of the site had a really specific vision for how he wanted information introduced and the flow of each different section on the webpage that he wanted structured and linked together.

And so then it was up to us doing the research and writing to follow his vision and also to bring up ideas when we thought that presenting an idea in a particular way might be a little bit off-putting to the audience we were trying to reach.
So being able to have those conversations in kind of an editorial aspect as well.
Yeah.
Yeah.
So I can go into more detail in any of those.
Whatever would be helpful to hear.
Yeah.

So I'm curious, like, when you think about the content you've produced and that writing side, like, especially when somebody's super particular, how do you think about, like, definition of done and, like, delivery sort of metrics?

Especially, mean, and I guess I'm curious also, like, balancing, maybe you haven't done this as much, but maybe, like, a little bit of a guesstimate question of, like, balancing at scale that, like, quality versus efficiency piece.

Yeah, so the quality and efficiency piece is something that I actually do a lot of in my current role, because we edit anywhere between, you know, 10 pieces if it's a longer production content that we're looking at in a day to, you know, I've done, like, 300 content.
Pieces a week, depending on what the project's timeline is.
We'll get some estimated quotas from our leadership, but they'll also usually give us like a deadline for when we need to get through content.
And so then we pace ourselves through that.
And, you know, ideal world, we would try and get it perfect and polished for every single thing.
But that can't always happen to, you know, dive into every little nuance of tone in a piece.

So it's having a sense that you develop, I think, over time with a client of what good enough looks like, that it's still really high quality content, but that you're moving things along at a reasonable pace.
We don't have as much of a feedback loop in my current role, but when I was writing for that research-based website, it was a really iterative process.
So it would be pretty frequent communication where I would be putting together research and findings and communicating direction that that was going.
So, So, yeah.
So, yeah.
Your director wanted to be really hands-on with some of that guidance.

And then as I was writing, sending over polished pieces, you know, that had appropriate grammar, spelling, and were, you know, formatted in a really nice way, and getting feedback on if there were specific gaps that they were noticing that they wanted addressed, or if there were slight adjustments, maybe research that he knew about with his background that I hadn't come across right away.
And so making sure that that feedback was taken into account and applied.
And so when it comes to something being done, that component of your question, I think a lot of it comes down to understanding the goals of each particular project.
It's like the content that I do now, done looks like making sure that we've caught all of the violations and doing quick fixes and then getting that out the door.
Whereas done for my other client, when I was doing that longer writing, was a much longer process to get things really polished.
He would send it out.
For other people to look at and give feedback.
And was okay with it being a very rolling, ongoing, editorial journey for each piece of content.
Awesome.
Can I, I guess I'm like trying to think of the best way to ask without violating NDA things.
I can dance around it.
Yeah.
How, talk to me about your AI experience, like how you think about using it in the space of content generation.
Um, what, maybe what you've learned and what surprises you, what's interesting in that space.
Yeah.
Um, I mean, I can talk a little bit about work capacity and also in my personal life.
Um, I have learned a lot in my current role around how AI pulls content from the web.
Um, like what kinds of things it's looking for in the different inputs that it's likely to draw on.
Um, I don't have a lot of hands-on experience curating content specifically targeted for SEO for like.
A variety of different brands.
But what I've seen in my AI experience is how content now needs to be developed in those really rich little snippets for all of that content to get pulled.
Because people aren't just searching Google.
They're also looking at AI models for answers and advice that can direct them towards different products.
And so that's been a really interesting piece to look at.
And in my own life, I've done some fun creative projects.
I use AI for graphic design bits, but also written content.
I enjoy it as a writing assistant.
I have found that most of my success comes from giving it really solid examples of the kind of writing that I wanted to create and emulate.
And then sometimes adjusting that and reiterating over time to get it closer and closer.
And often asking for a couple different sample pieces and then pulling together the best three elements.
Those responses that I like to create something that feels more cohesive.
I don't think it's quite at the point where it can create something entirely seamless from, you know, a first prompt to production.
But I really enjoy it as a way to brainstorm and get some more ideas on how to approach, how to frame content.
You know, more than just a thesaurus, but it can actually create whole different paragraphs to see how ideas might come together.
Yeah, our CEO, like, if you watch him put together, like, a company brief, like, for context to build artifacts on, like, nobody really got this reference, but I keep using it.
I feel like it's like making cheese where you're like, you know, if you use, like, the cheesecloth and you keep, like, filtering it through and then, like, you make, like, a ball of mozzarella.
Nobody likes that reference, but the way I think of it is, like, you just, like, keep pouring stuff through and, like, molding it and then you're like, okay, well, now it all
Well, now it's all sticking together.
Right, right.
And, like, it takes all those different layers for it to finally start becoming, like, something that's not just a glob on the side.
Exactly.
Yeah, yeah, I get it.
Well, so what are you, like, looking for next?
I mean, you've done, like, a couple of different things, and they do, like, fall within the areas of focus for this role.
But, like, what are you hoping for?
Yeah, I would really love something that gives me a mix of, you know, direct person-to-person contact.
My current role is fairly siloed.
I've created a network within the company I work for.
But I really enjoy the human element of getting to know, you know, coworkers and also clients.
Bringing my writing and editorial skills, it's a piece I really love and was part of the reason that I didn't stick with the ski realm.
As much as I loved being out with people and teaching kids.
And also that behind-the-scenes problem-solving.-scenes-solving.
problem-solving.
you.
And I'm happy to give you more examples.
I don't want to take up too much more time.
But I really enjoy digging into glitches in internal processes that might be slowing things down, offering like tips and tricks.
I was joking with some co-workers the other day.
was sharing some advice on tools that I had figured out how to use to like half my work time on a project that we're working on right now.
And they were kind of laughing and asking how I had come up with it.
And my joke was that I'm secretly just a little bit lazy, which isn't actually true.
I'm quite motivated, but I love finding efficient ways because then I can take my creativity and energy different directions.
You know, it gets a little bit of a chuckle.
Totally.
I mean, I would love to hear an example of something that you've like fixed or a process that you've optimized.
Yeah, totally.
So, I mean, literally just today, we are working on a task where we're pulling content from a couple different models for a comparison.
And when that content gets pulled, it has some really specific markdown formatting.
I don't know if you're familiar with that technical language.
The markdown formatting doesn't always transfer the correct way.
And so part of my job right now is to go through and really fine-tooth comb, look for all those little markdown errors, which can be kind of mind-numbing to spot when you are in a 20-page document.

And so I have figured out a way with some collaboration with some other coworkers on how to use regular expressions within our documentation to create a really quick search that highlights a lot of common errors that we see.
So that instead of reading line by line, we can actually scroll through more visually.
And, you know, it takes 10 minutes to do a quick glance over some of these common violations rather than an hour to read the whole document.
And that process was so effective, I shared it with our team leadership, who have put it into some of our resource documents.
And then yesterday, I offered...
Or to run a little workshop, essentially, for some of the newer folks on the task who came in.
They shared that across a couple different projects that are happening right now.
And so I presented some of these tips to about like 100 people that I work with yesterday.
Just to help get everybody up to speed and using some of these tools.
Awesome.
Cool.
Well, those are all my questions.
And we have exactly 10 minutes left.
So what questions do you have for me?
Yeah, I would love to hear a little bit about what you're looking for in terms of like success in like one month and six months.
And what kind of those that training and onboarding and like shifts in responsibility are going to look like getting up to speed.
Because I know we can have up to 10 clients in this role.
So I'd love to hear a little bit about that trajectory.
Yeah, I mean, honestly, this role, I think there's like five to eight.
Or like, well, five is what we're up.
I'm optimizing for right now, like, yes, maybe in six months, you might be at 10, but that is dependent on like the product scaling and all these other things.
So yeah, I would say like five, five is more what I would be thinking about.
But the in terms of like onboarding, I will say we have, we're getting better at onboarding and creating space for onboarding.
Um, and cause we have had like people where we're like, we're just so overwhelmed, please just start managing an account.

Um, and I, I think that's actually one of the nice things about this team is like, they are super scrappy and they like people who are still here have like been thrown into the deep end and wanted to figure it out.
Um, we won't throw you into the deep end.
That is the goal.
Um, but I think, you know, in terms of onboarding, we have moved.
we have have moved.
have have moved.
moved.
moved.
When I joined, we moved to a new model where all of our new accounts go into an eight-week implementation sprint.
So we call them strategy sprints, but it's basically to shape the lanes of work that we're going to do.
Because there are a couple of things that we can do.
We want to set up the client's workspace and the product really well.
And so I think as we onboard new Emmys and new people for pods, we'll probably start people in the strategy sprint.
It's really hands-on with like, it's the best of the best of the team.
And I think it's really useful to get an idea, like just to see, this is how we create the artifacts for the first time.
This is how we shape things, like oftentimes we're shaping things in like, clawed perplexity, manis, like what I was talking about, the cheesecloth.
Sorting things through, getting a really good version, and then saying, okay, this is how we did it.
Let's build a workflow for it, like in the Atlas is the name of our internal platform.
And so seeing that process, I think, is really enlightening.
It's all of our best practices that are starting to trickle down to the rest of the teams.
But starting there is, I think, really beneficial.
And then, you know, based on resourcing and how things go in terms of like conversion to long-term clients, things like that.
Like there would be an opportunity to start like going into a pod with more like consistent client base.
And, yeah, so pods, the way that the pods sit, there's like two MEs under an account strategist or director.
And then there
There are two to three CMs working in a pod with an ME.
So like Emmys, directors have like eight to 10 accounts, Emmys have three to five, and CMs have like one to two because they're like generating the day-to-day content.
Makes sense.
So yeah, like you could end up in like a pod with a longer term support.
And I think like in terms, if this question is about like career growth, I will say like we are less, we are a year old pretty much.
Like we just did our first like renewals in July.
And so like, you know, the rising tide lifts all boats sort of thing.
If we're really successful at growing our account base and our platform like become something that it might become...
Like its own SaaS product where clients can access it.
Like there are a lot of different directions and I can't say like, okay, we're going to have a hundred clients at the end of the year.
so what does that look like from a team perspective?
But I would say in the first, like the first month would be probably two months would be shadowing strategy sprints and then starting to like take on clients as they kick off.
Um, and like being paired with an ME and, and helping do that work, um, and then owning that.
Um, and then, you know, depending on how we're looking in terms of, um, launching, like, again, converting clients long-term, um, possibly moving you into, um, uh, a pod to manage clients long-term.
Does that answer your question?
I guess like.
It does.
Yeah, no, that, that hit kind of all the different layers.
I was, you know, curious a little bit about like employee experience, um, growth.
I know as a new company, I appreciate your candor.
I've been in roles that have over-promised, and you're like, oh man.
And I'm familiar with learning the ropes of a new company.
So the role I'm in now didn't exist two years ago.
I've been here about a year and a half.
And so it's been a lot of learning on the fly.
And the things I was doing a year ago are wildly different than what I'm doing now.
And that's part of the fun of it.
It's part of the challenge of it.
But yeah, I'm very familiar with kind of being in that startup-adjacent realm.
Yeah.
One other question.
I snooped a little on your LinkedIn and saw that you're fairly new, from what I could tell, with GrowthX.
I'd love to hear a little bit about what caught your eye about the company and what you've appreciated so far being there.
Yeah.
So some work, like people that I've networked
With their, like, what is it, like, two degrees of separation or something, like, work here.
And so I got intros because they were looking for, like, customer ops.
So I wasn't aware of GrowthX until GrowthX found me.
But I think, like, the thing that is so fascinating about it, I came from SaaS, like, I've worked in SaaS.
mean, I also, like, having worked at Uber, it's a little bit more of, like, a services model.
But the services model is so, like, such a smart way to approach, like, the business problem of building a product.
Because, like, right, you essentially, MEs and CMs are, like, our UX research.
Like, we're getting the customer feedback because they're using the tool every day.
And we have a lot of access to kind of, like, what they're doing in the tool and how they're using it and what's working and not working.
And, Yeah.
And, yeah.

And so sometimes that makes it more urgent, but also like, but it's also like an incredible learning opportunity for like the engineering team to be able to, like, we've just, it's, the product is two months old and it's, it's so powerful.
And I think the ways that it's changed are already incredible.
Um, and so, but like, we're not funding that part through VC, right?
We're funding that, like, we have paying customers who are paying us like they would pay a marketing agency.

Um, and so we get to like, do the work and do the deliverables and, and like, if there's a giant bug in the product, we go to Claude, like, you know, and there were periods of time where we were working in Claude before, you know, and ChatGPT before this product was live because for a variety of reasons.
Um, so like, I think that the model just makes so much sense in terms of
Like the growth potential and the like long-term viability of the company and the product.
Like there are so many different ways that like AI is going and we kind of don't have to make a really public bet yet on like what core problem the product is going to solve.
Like we're going after a couple of different things, right?
Um, but we can hedge a little bit and say like, okay, like this is what it needs to do for our team.
But like people are talking about like how you show up in LLM search.
People are also talking about like, you know, there are so many conversations happening about and like micro products that are solving these problems.
And we get to kind of observe and, and make bets in a more like intelligent and less like showy way, which I think is smart.
LLM because here's turns Well
Yeah, that makes a lot of sense.

It's a little more behind the scenes and driven by what you're seeing in terms of like customer interaction and requirements and, you know, larger scale where AI is developing because who has a crystal ball for that, right?

Right, yeah, like you don't have to go be like making, that's the thing I hate about tech is like the broad proclamations people make because they need money and so they need everybody to believe that this is what's going to happen next.
And like, we don't have to, I mean, we have to do that to some extent because people are asking us what we think, but we also don't have to be like, our product solves this problem.
Right, right.
Yeah, that totally makes sense.
Yeah, super helpful to hear.
Thanks.
Yeah.
I know we're basically out of time.
My just last curiosity was sort of next steps in the process.
I know there were a few more interview components and that I did the practical exercise first.
Just want to make sure I know kind of where this is going.
Yeah, so you've only talked to me.
Aurelia, right?
Yes.
Yeah.
So I think you're talking to one more person, I think Panzer, and then who will be more on, like, the technical workflow side.
Like, he's in the tool a lot more than I am.
And then after that, the second exercise would be, like, presenting on what you first did.
And I'll, like, say we were honing that.
Like, the directions have been interpreted widely.
I imagine.

But really, it's an opportunity for us to look at, like, the thought process behind how you developed your writing sample and how you would talk about that writing sample to a client, not necessarily, like, in presentation format.
But, like, what are the things that stood out to you as you were doing that research and how you think about, like, the writing itself?
giving examples?
Like,
Makes sense.
Cool.
Yeah.
All right.
Well, it was really nice to meet you.
Thank you for your time.
Yeah, was nice to meet you, too.
Yes, I hope you get something to eat.
I hope we're not totally ruining our I snacked a little bit earlier, so I know.
Thanks.
All right.
Yeah.
No, I really appreciate chatting.
And, yeah, if you have any outstanding questions or anything, happy to answer via email or whatever.
Awesome.
Thanks, Katya.
Have a good day.
You, too.
Bye.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Hiring manager interview with Katya Luscomb for the Technical Managing Editor role at GrowthX.](https://fathom.video/share/WCF-6zqx3Qhqk1vous2LupuKnyM_c3-X?tab=summary&timestamp=378.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [Role involves project management and platform optimization for GrowthX's AI content generation service](https://fathom.video/share/WCF-6zqx3Qhqk1vous2LupuKnyM_c3-X?tab=summary&timestamp=426.0)
  - [Katya has diverse experience in content creation, editing, and project management](https://fathom.video/share/WCF-6zqx3Qhqk1vous2LupuKnyM_c3-X?tab=summary&timestamp=716.0)
  - [GrowthX is a rapidly growing startup with a unique services-based approach to product development](https://fathom.video/share/WCF-6zqx3Qhqk1vous2LupuKnyM_c3-X?tab=summary&timestamp=583.0)
  - [Position offers potential for growth as the company and product evolve](https://fathom.video/share/WCF-6zqx3Qhqk1vous2LupuKnyM_c3-X?tab=summary&timestamp=652.0)

## Topics

### Role Overview

  - [Part of customer operations team, organizing delivery processes](https://fathom.video/share/WCF-6zqx3Qhqk1vous2LupuKnyM_c3-X?tab=summary&timestamp=436.0)
  - [Two main responsibilities:](https://fathom.video/share/WCF-6zqx3Qhqk1vous2LupuKnyM_c3-X?tab=summary&timestamp=478.0)
      - [Project management (client-facing and internal)](https://fathom.video/share/WCF-6zqx3Qhqk1vous2LupuKnyM_c3-X?tab=summary&timestamp=484.0)
      - [Platform optimization and feedback for GrowthX's proprietary AI tool](https://fathom.video/share/WCF-6zqx3Qhqk1vous2LupuKnyM_c3-X?tab=summary&timestamp=583.0)
  - [Works within pods: Account Director, Account Strategist, Content Manager](https://fathom.video/share/WCF-6zqx3Qhqk1vous2LupuKnyM_c3-X?tab=summary&timestamp=499.0)
  - [Manages 5-8 clients initially, potentially scaling to 10](https://fathom.video/share/WCF-6zqx3Qhqk1vous2LupuKnyM_c3-X?tab=summary&timestamp=1490.0)

### Candidate Background

  - [Current: Content analyst/editor for large tech AI company](https://fathom.video/share/WCF-6zqx3Qhqk1vous2LupuKnyM_c3-X?tab=summary&timestamp=723.0)
  - [Previous experience:](https://fathom.video/share/WCF-6zqx3Qhqk1vous2LupuKnyM_c3-X?tab=summary&timestamp=716.0)
      - [Outdoor education project management](https://fathom.video/share/WCF-6zqx3Qhqk1vous2LupuKnyM_c3-X?tab=summary&timestamp=756.0)
      - [Hiring team management (60 seasonal hires)](https://fathom.video/share/WCF-6zqx3Qhqk1vous2LupuKnyM_c3-X?tab=summary&timestamp=771.0)
      - [Journalism (Pittsburgh Post-Gazette)](https://fathom.video/share/WCF-6zqx3Qhqk1vous2LupuKnyM_c3-X?tab=summary&timestamp=813.0)
      - [In-depth research writing for psychology/well-being website](https://fathom.video/share/WCF-6zqx3Qhqk1vous2LupuKnyM_c3-X?tab=summary&timestamp=844.0)
  - [Strong skills in editorial processes, research, and efficiency optimization](https://fathom.video/share/WCF-6zqx3Qhqk1vous2LupuKnyM_c3-X?tab=summary&timestamp=840.0)

### AI Experience and Perspective

  - [Familiar with AI content generation and web scraping processes](https://fathom.video/share/WCF-6zqx3Qhqk1vous2LupuKnyM_c3-X?tab=summary&timestamp=1119.0)
  - [Personal experience using AI for creative projects and as a writing assistant](https://fathom.video/share/WCF-6zqx3Qhqk1vous2LupuKnyM_c3-X?tab=summary&timestamp=1165.0)
